exemplar.title: How does the Galaxy Morphology Relate to the Star Formation Regions of Galaxies?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does the Galaxy Morphology Relate to the Star Formation Regions of Galaxies?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Topic is communicated clearly and revisited consistently throughout the introduction, hypothesis and conclusion.
Relevant astrophysical context (density-wave theory, Jeans criterion) is provided to support the purpose of the investigation.
Research question is explicitly stated and maintained, showing a coherent investigative focus.
weaknesses: Research question remains broad and non-quantitative, limiting the precision of subsequent analysis.
Methodology lacks justification of sample selection (galaxy choice and size) and omits key calibration steps.
Occasional digressions and imprecise wording reduce the overall sharpness of focus.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs investigation sustains a clear scientific purpose and consistently references the research question, meeting the upper half of the 3â4 band for Focus and Method. However, the scope of the question is too broad and methodological details (sample justification, calibration frames) are incomplete, preventing a 5â6 band score. A score of 4 appropriately reflects strong communication and structure but moderate limitations in methodological rigor and question precision.

strengths: Demonstrates robust subject knowledge, accurately explaining Jeans instability, density waves and H-alpha emission.
Terminology (e.g., spectral energy distribution, H-alpha flux) is used consistently and effectively to support analysis.
Diagrams and cited sources underpin conceptual explanations and link theory to observations.
weaknesses: Occasional terminology errors (e.g., âphysiciansâ instead of âphysicistsâ) and inconsistent notation.
Minor factual and numerical inaccuracies (galaxy identifiers, temperature bounds in Table 1).
Firstâmention citations and table notations need clarification for full academic precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay shows strong command of astrophysical concepts and generally precise use of technical vocabulary, meriting a score at the top of the 3â4 band. Persistent but infrequent lapses in terminology, citation practice and numerical accuracy justify elevating the score into the 5â6 range without reaching the flawless standard of the top band. A score of 5 acknowledges excellent knowledge application tempered by these errors.

strengths: Argument is logically structured from theoretical framework through data presentation to conclusion.
Effective integration of H-alpha imaging with density-wave theory highlights the relationship between morphology and star-formation.
The evaluation section identifies practical limitations and reflects on acquired observational skills.
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely qualitative; lacks quantitative metrics (flux measurements, statistical tests) to substantiate comparisons.
Critical evaluation does not fully explore alternative explanations or propose concrete methodological improvements.
Imageâprocessing decisions (channel combination, aberration effects) are not examined in depth for their impact on results.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student consistently analyzes observations in light of the research question and develops a coherent argument, placing the work solidly in the 7â9 band for Critical Thinking. The integration of theory and practice is strong, but the absence of rigorous quantitative treatment, limited critical exploration of alternatives and only cursory evaluation of error sources prevent reaching the exceptional 10â12 band. A score of 10 reflects very good critical thinking with room for deeper evaluation and measurement.

strengths: Clear overall structure with a functional table of contents, section headings and consistent page numbering.
Citation style is applied consistently, aiding navigation and reference.
Sections flow logically, supporting reader comprehension of the investigative process.
weaknesses: Figures display inconsistent formatting (caption styles, resolution, labeling) and occasional Markdown/LaTeX artefacts.
Layout and placement of images sometimes interrupt the text flow and lack scale/orientation annotations.
Citation list mixes styles and ordering, and sentenceâlevel formatting errors (fragments, informal tone) detract from formality.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation is organised and follows standard conventions, earning placement in the 3â4 band for Structure and Formatting. However, persistent inconsistencies in figure presentation, mixed citation styles and minor formalâstyle errors reduce effectiveness, placing the work at the lower end of the 3â4 band. A score of 3 captures clear structure combined with notable layout shortcomings.

strandSelections.reasoning: Specialist terminologyâH-alpha emission, density waves, Jeans mass, spectral energy distributionâis used frequently and usually correctly, indicating a reasonable grasp of astrophysical vocabulary. Some lapses (e.g., âphysiciansâ for âphysicists,â inconsistent capitalisation, occasional misuse such as âgalactical matterâ) detract from precision and consistency. These errors suggest competent but not flawless usage, corresponding to the middle of the 3-4 band.

The argument follows a coherent structure: theoretical framework â data presentation â comparison â conclusion. Connections between morphology, gas density, and star-formation tracers are logically developed, and the conclusion is aligned with presented evidence. Nevertheless, the reasoning is occasionally repetitive, lacks critical engagement with alternative explanations (e.g., interaction-driven starbursts), and relies heavily on visual inspection rather than measured quantities, limiting sophistication. These attributes reflect a solid but not advanced argument, meriting a mid-band score (4-6).

The student outlines a step-by-step data-collection procedure (use of university telescope, V and H-alpha filters, image stacking in Siril) and supplements primary images with secondary NASA data for inaccessible galaxies. While these choices are broadly appropriate, the range is limited (only four galaxies, two of which are archival) and lacks justification of sample size, exposure settings, calibration frames, or error-reduction strategies. Evidence of informed selection is partial rather than thorough, aligning with the mid-band (3-4) descriptors.

The essay demonstrates **solid subject knowledge**: key astrophysical principles (Jeans instability, Wienâs law, Balmer series, density-wave theory) are explained and linked to observations. Sources are cited and diagrams support explanations. Occasional oversimplifications (e.g., treating all spiral galaxies as uniformly young; minor numerical inaccuracies) prevent the analysis from reaching exemplary sophistication, but the overall command of Physics concepts is clear and generally accurate, justifying the upper end of the 3-4 band.

The evaluation section identifies practical limitations (telescope alignment, focus variability, weather constraints) and briefly reflects on skills gained. However, it does not quantify uncertainties, propose specific methodological improvements, or critically assess the validity of conclusions drawn from such a small, partly archival sample. The critical perspective is present but limited in depth, aligning with the lower end of the 4-6 band.

The studentâs topicâinvestigating the relationship between galaxy morphology and star-formation regionsâis stated prominently in the title, introduction and hypothesis, and is revisited in the conclusion. The focus on spiral versus elliptical galaxies and H-alpha emission gives the investigation a recognizable scientific purpose that is maintained throughout. Explanatory context (e.g., density-wave theory, Jeans criterion) is provided, allowing a reader to understand why the question is worthwhile. Minor issues in wording (e.g., âHow does the Galaxy Morphology Relateâ¦â) and occasional digressions reduce precision, but overall the topic is communicated with sufficient clarity and consistency to meet the top of the 3-4 band.

Images are plentiful and generally relevant, but their resolution, scaling and colour balance are inconsistent; some figures are reused from secondary sources without clear differentiation from primary data. Captions often duplicate alt-text descriptions, and placement occasionally interrupts text flow. While the layout is serviceable, these inconsistencies and the mixture of Markdown artefacts with PDF-style elements reveal only **basic** presentational effectiveness, corresponding to the 1-2 bandâs upper level.

The research question is explicitly stated and recurs in the hypothesis and conclusion, showing a genuine attempt at focus. Nevertheless, it remains rather broad for an EEâlinking an entire class of morphologies to star-formation without specifying quantitative parameters (e.g., SFR indicators, surface densities, sample size) limits the sharpness of the focus. Connections to the ensuing discussion are present but occasionally general or descriptive rather than tightly analytical, placing the work solidly in the middle of the 3-4 band.

The student analyses images by identifying H-alpha hotspots in spiral versus elliptical galaxies and links these patterns to theoretical expectations (density waves, gas content). While the discussion is more qualitative than quantitative and based on a very small data set, it consistently addresses the research question and integrates theory with observations. Depth is moderate rather than comprehensiveâno rigorous statistical treatment or error analysis is providedâplacing the work comfortably in the 4-6 band at about the mid-point.

The essay is organised into a clear sequence of sections (Introduction, Theory, Methodology, Data, Conclusion, Evaluation, Citations) supported by a functional table of contents and page numbering. Headings, sub-headings and internal referencing enable straightforward navigation. Citation style is applied consistently. Minor formatting glitches (e.g., mixed Markdown/LaTeX artefacts, occasional inconsistent figure numbering) exist but do not impede comprehension, warranting a score at the top of the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: Combining V and H-alpha channels into one image obscures individual contributions; present separate channel maps or color composites for transparent analysis.
Misuse of âphysiciansâ instead of âphysicistsâ when referring to theoretical work introduces inaccurate terminology; correct to maintain subjectâspecific precision.
Figure reference formatting is inconsistent (âFigure 1.â vs âFigure 1:â); standardize caption style and numbering for uniform presentation.
Word count is provided and within the 4000-word limit, meeting the formatting requirement.
The title âPhysicsExtended Essayâ lacks spacing and a descriptive subtitle; separate words and include the investigationâs full title for clearer presentation.
The discussion of Hubbleâs classification credits Hubble but lacks a direct citation at first mention; include a source for Hubbleâs original work to support the historical context.

The research question is stated clearly but remains broad; refine its scope by specifying quantitative parameters (e.g., SFR density or sample size) to strengthen focus and guide analysis more precisely.
Typographical error in âJeanâs criteriaâ; the correct term is âJeansâ criterion.â Consistent terminology is essential for precision.
List defines Ïâ though equation (1) uses Ï; clarify whether Ïâ and Ï are identical or correct notation to avoid confusion.
The table of contents is clearly formatted with all major and minor sections listed alongside page numbers, facilitating easy navigation.
Works cited list mixes citation styles and ordering; adopt a consistent format (e.g., APA) and alphabetize entries to improve presentation quality.
The phrase beginning with âSuch as the different typesâ¦â is a sentence fragment; integrate it into the preceding sentence for clearer and more coherent writing.
The introduction outlines context well but does not identify a specific research gap; include a statement on how this study builds on or fills a gap in existing literature.
The proposed method is described generally but lacks justification for sample choice and size; specify criteria for selecting the four galaxies to underpin methodological rigor.
Figure 2 caption is overly verbose within the main text; distill the caption to key points and retain extended description only in alt text for clarity.
Linking massive star lifetimes directly to supernova-driven SFRs requires discussion of feedback mechanisms; expand analysis to include shock-induced collapse processes.
The claim that elliptical galaxies âlack galactic matterâ relies on a non-peer-reviewed source; strengthen evaluation by citing observational studies or surveys of gas content.
SED comparison is described qualitatively; include quantitative metrics (e.g., flux ratios) to substantiate differences in optical vs. infrared emission.
The statement that SFRs âoccurred 10â12 billion years agoâ misrepresents ongoing star formation; revise to reflect that SFRs are active in present-day galaxies.
In Table 1, the âMâ type row lists a temperature range starting at 1 Kâlikely a typographical error; verify and correct the temperature bounds and corresponding wavelength values.
H-alpha emission is explained qualitatively; include a ÎE calculation for the n=3ân=2 transition to demonstrate why the wavelength is 656.6 nm.
Acknowledges non-ideal f/3.8 focal ratio but omits discussion of optical aberrations; evaluate how this choice affected image quality and data accuracy.
Hypothesis is clear but non-quantitative; define expected difference in SFR density or H-alpha flux between spiral and elliptical samples.
Figure 5 lacks scale, orientation, and labels for filters; include annotations (e.g., north arrow, filter identifiers) to improve clarity.
Methodology omits essential calibration steps (dark, bias, flat-field corrections); incorporate these procedures to ensure robust error reduction.
Image analysis is limited to visual inspection; quantify H-alpha emission (e.g., flux measurements) to enable objective comparison of SFR density.
Effective integration of H-alpha imaging with density-wave theory clearly illustrates how morphology influences star-formation regions.
Section title â5.2 Data 3â mismatches sequence; correct numbering (âData 2â) to maintain logical structure.
Text compares M51âs SFRs to âNGC 1275â instead of M33; correct galaxy identifiers to preserve analytical accuracy.
The summary asserts spirals evolve into ellipticals through matter consumption without supporting citation; reference galactic evolution studies to underpin this claim.
First-person phrasing (âI wishâ¦â) and informal tone undermine academic style; revise to third-person language for consistency with formal reporting.
Evaluation identifies limitations but lacks targeted improvement strategies (e.g., optimal exposure settings, error metrics); propose concrete adjustments for future observations.
